context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 12th February, 2024
Days Covered: 01 and 02 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)

Please make note of the following instructions:
- Your solutions must be submitted to Gradescope as a single PDF file. While we allow handwritten solutions, we strongly suggest that you typeset in LaTeX, using the template available on Canvas.
- Each problem must be accompanied by a collaboration statement: the students you collaborated with, and any external resources you consulted when solving the problem. Do not leave this blank—instead write "collaborators: none" if appropriate. See the Course Information handout for more details on the collaboration policy.


Problem 1. Collaboration Policy [10 points]

Carefully read the collaboration policy in our Course Information Handout. Then, consider the scenarios described below, and discuss whether and why each one does or does not violate the course homework policies. If the issue is ambiguous, explain how you would resolve it. Simply answering "I'd check with the TA" for each answer is not sufficient.

(a) You host a pset party where ten of you work together to solve the problems before separating to write up your own solutions.
(b) You work with a friend on a problem, taking turns writing ideas and calculations jointly in the same notebook as you work out the solution, then photograph those notes so you can each have a copy of your sketch before you separate to write up your own solutions.
(c) You work together to figure out a detailed solution; one of you then dictates it to the other so that each of you can turn in a solution you've written yourself.
(d) After you've solved a problem, your friend who is still working on the problem asks you for feedback on a solution idea. Leveraging your knowledge of the right answer, you identify and point out a flaw in their proposed solution.
(e) After you've solved a problem, you try to help your friends by acting as a sounding board while they work towards the solution. With the deadline approaching, since they are still stuck, you allow them to skim your writeup to get a general idea of how to approach the problem, which allows them to go off and independently figure out their solution and write it up.
(f) After you've worked with your friend to solve a problem and each done your own writeup, you exchange writeups and examine them to identify and correct any flaws in one or the other.
(g) You remember seeing the homework problem in a problem set of a class you took previously. You look at your notes, see that you got a perfect grade, and copy your solution.
(h) You know the answer to the problem because you read it in a book you are reading to complement the required textbook, so you write it down that way.
(i) You know the answer to the problem because you read it in a book you are reading to complement the required textbook, so you explain the solution to your pset collaborators.
(j) While completing a late problem set for partial credit, you read the published solutions as you type your own answers, making sure to adjust the word choice and sentence structure as you go.


Problem 2. Set Formulas and Propositional Formulas [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] Use a truth table to verify that the propositional formula (P AND NOT Q) OR (P AND Q) is equivalent to P.
Note: For propositions, the three notations "NOT Q", "¬Q", and "Q-bar" are interchangeable.

(b) [5 pts] Prove that
    A = (A − B) ∪ (A ∩ B)
for all sets A, B, by showing
    x ∈ A   iff   x ∈ ((A − B) ∪ (A ∩ B))
for all elements x. How does this relate to part (a)?
Note: the set difference A − B is defined on page 105.


Problem 3. Predicate Practice [10 points]

For parts (a)–(d) below, translate the given statements into predicate logic, while preserving the meaning as closely as possible. In addition to boolean logic symbols like AND, OR, NOT, and IMPLIES, and quantifiers ∃ and ∀, you may build predicates using arithmetic, inequalities, and constants.

For this problem, you should use ℕ = {0, 1, 2, ...} as your domain of discourse: all variables are assumed to belong to ℕ, so ∃x and ∀x mean ∃x ∈ ℕ and ∀x ∈ ℕ. Avoid quantifying over other sets, such as "∀p ∈ Primes" or "∃n ≥ 1".

For example, "n is odd" could be translated as
    isOdd(n) := ∃m. (2m + 1 = n)
or, relatedly,
    isNotEven(n) := NOT (∃a. n = 2a).
The predicate "p is a prime number" could be translated to
    isPrime(p) := (p > 1) AND NOT (∃m ∃n. (m > 1 AND n > 1 AND mn = p)).
You may use the predicates isOdd(n) and isPrime(p) by name in your answers below.

(a) [2 pts] The predicate sixish(n) defined as "n is a natural number whose first digit is 6."
    Hint: You may wish to use exponents, e.g., 10^k. Be careful about where you do or don't need quantifiers.
(b) [2 pts] "There are exactly two natural numbers n such that Q(n) is true."
(c) [2 pts] Goldbach's Conjecture: "every even integer greater than 2 can be written as the sum of two primes."
    Note: Do not use quantifier shorthand like "∀n > 2. V(n)". Instead, use the more basic "∀n. W(n)" and include all necessary logic inside W(n).
    Hint: "If n is even and greater than 2, then ...".
(d) [2 pts] "Every prime larger than 100 is sixish."
    Hint: You may refer to "sixish" by name, even if you didn't solve part (a).
(e) [2 pts] Is the predicate R(n) := "∃b. n = 2b + 5" equivalent to isOdd(n)? Prove your answer.
    Hint: Recall that our domain of discourse is ℕ.


Problem 4. Induction and Contradiction [Edited] [20 points]

(a) [10 pts] Suppose Q(x) is a predicate defined for all x ∈ ℝ. Assume Q has the following property: for every x, y ∈ ℝ, if Q(x · y) is true, then at least one of Q(x) or Q(y) must be true.
From this, prove that Q satisfies the following stronger property: for any integer n ≥ 1 and any n numbers x1, x2, ..., xn ∈ ℝ, if Q(x1 · x2 · ... · xn) is true, then at least one of Q(x1), Q(x2), ..., Q(xn) must be true.
Prove the result by induction on n. Be sure to carefully identify your inductive hypothesis P(n), your base case(s), and the assumption and goal in your inductive step.
    Hint: x1 · x2 · ... · x_{n−1} · xn = (x1 · x2 · ... · x_{n−1}) · xn

(b) [5 pts] Consider the following theorem:
    Theorem 1. If the product of n ≥ 1 real numbers is irrational, then at least one of those numbers must be irrational.
We will eventually prove this Theorem by citing part (a). In preparation, what should we choose for Q from part (a)? What lemma must we prove about your chosen predicate Q before we're allowed to use part (a)?

(c) [5 pts] Use contradiction to carefully prove the lemma that you identified in part (b), and then finish the proof of Theorem 1.
While proving your lemma, you should not assume any prior knowledge about irrational numbers beyond the definition ("irrational" means "not rational"), but you may use familiar facts about rational numbers.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on Problem 1, especially how detailed the “does/does not violate policy” explanations are supposed to be. Could you just explain what they’re really looking for overall before I try the individual parts?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct to get oriented first. The assignment asks you to evaluate each scenario against the collaboration policy — which you should have read carefully. The policy has two distinct phases it cares about: *solving* and *writing up*. Keep those in mind as your framework.

So before diving into the scenarios: in your own words, what does the policy say is allowed vs. not allowed in each of those two phases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for general orientation before diving in, which is a reasonable starting request. I should help them understand what to focus on without doing the analysis for them. I'll point them back to the key elements of the collaboration policy they need to apply, and ask them a guiding question to get them started rather than giving a roadmap of the answers.

exchanges.student: I’m not totally sure, which is the problem. I think “solving together” is sort of allowed, but “writing together” isn’t, and maybe looking at anything too detailed is bad? I’m fuzzy on where shared notes or checking each other’s writeups fits, and I kind of want the big-picture version first.
exchanges.tutor:
You're on the right track — the solving/writing distinction is exactly the right framework. But rather than me summarizing it, the policy is right there in the assignment handout. Go re-read the 'Solving' and 'Writing' paragraphs carefully.

After that, tell me: what's the *maximum* group size allowed for collaborative solving, and what does the policy say your writeup must reflect?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right general instinct but is still fuzzy on the details. Rather than giving them the big-picture summary they're asking for, I should push them to re-read the policy and extract the key distinctions themselves. I'll affirm what they got right and ask a targeted question to sharpen their understanding. The policy is right there in the assignment — I should direct them to it.

exchanges.student: I think it said something like 3 or 4 people for solving, and the writeup has to be in my own words and show my own understanding. But I’m still not confident, because the examples all feel borderline to me and I keep overthinking them. Could you maybe give me a general way to judge the scenarios without going one by one yet?
exchanges.tutor:
You've got the essentials right — group size limit and writeup independence. That's your framework.

The best way to stop overthinking is to just try one. Pick whichever scenario feels most straightforward to you, apply those two criteria, and tell me what you think. We can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified the two key elements from the policy. They're asking for a general judging framework before tackling scenarios, which risks me doing the analytical work for them. I should affirm what they got right, and nudge them to try applying it to a specific scenario rather than continuing to seek a meta-framework. The best way to build confidence is to start working through examples, not to keep abstracting.

exchanges.student: Okay, but before I pick one, I’m worried I’m missing hidden rules. Like, is the main test basically “did they collaborate during solving only, and then fully separate before writing,” or are there more subtle red flags I should be watching for too? I’d rather have the whole checklist in my head first.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but at this point the best thing is to just try one. You already have enough to start — if you're missing something, we'll catch it as we go.

Pick a scenario and give me your analysis. Which one feels most clear-cut to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking for a complete checklist before attempting anything — this is a pattern of avoidance rather than genuine confusion. They've been asking variations of the same meta-question for several exchanges now. I should firmly but kindly redirect them to just attempt a scenario. The policy is there to read; my job isn't to summarize it for them. I should be direct that continuing to seek a framework without trying is unproductive.

exchanges.student: Maybe (a), but I honestly don’t know how much explanation is enough. I feel like I could say “10 people is too many, so it violates the policy,” but that seems way too short, and then I’m back to wanting a fuller explanation of what kind of justification they expect.
exchanges.tutor: You've identified something real — group size is relevant. But is that the *only* potential issue in scenario (a)? Think about what else the policy says around collaborative solving before you conclude.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable starting analysis for (a) and is now worrying about length/detail of justification rather than the substance. I should affirm that they're on the right track and push them to think about whether their analysis is complete — specifically whether group size is the *only* issue in scenario (a), rather than worrying about format. This moves them forward substantively.

exchanges.student: That didn’t really clear it up for me, so I’m trying a different angle: could you show me what a strong answer for one scenario would look like in terms of structure, not necessarily the exact final wording? I think I’m stuck more on the style of reasoning than the content.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't model an answer structure for you — that's your job to figure out as you write. What I can say is: you already have a substantive point about (a). Don't worry about style right now.

Here's a sharper question: in scenario (a), after the ten-person party, does everyone separate and write independently? Does the policy say anything about *that* part of the process?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to model a complete answer structure for them, which would essentially do the work for them and violate the 'never provide complete or partial solutions' rule. I also can't write or suggest language for them. I should decline clearly but redirect productively — pointing out that they already have a substantive start on (a) and pushing them to develop it further themselves.

exchanges.student: Yes, they do separate and write independently, so that part seems okay to me. Then I get stuck because if the writeup part is okay, I’m not sure whether the answer is just “still violates because the solving group was too large,” or whether there’s some broader principle I’m supposed to talk about.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you've correctly identified that the writing part seems fine. So the violation hinges on the solving phase.

Think about *why* the policy sets a group size limit. What problem does a 10-person group create that a 3-4 person group doesn't? The policy actually hints at this directly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified both issues in (a): group size violates the policy, and the writeup part seems fine. They're asking whether group size alone is sufficient or if there's a broader principle. This is good progress — I should affirm the group size point and ask them to think about whether a large group creates any *other* concerns beyond just the number, to see if they can arrive at the reasoning themselves.

exchanges.student: I guess because bigger groups make participation uneven or make it easier for some people to just absorb ideas without really solving? I’m still feeling shaky though, and part of me wants to zoom out and just get a broad explanation of how to approach all of Problem 1 before I commit.
exchanges.tutor:
You've just answered your own question — that's exactly the reasoning behind the group size rule. You have what you need for (a).

I've noticed you keep pulling back to ask for a broader framework right when you're about to commit. That pattern isn't helping you. Write up your answer for (a) now, then we'll move to (b). Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified the reasoning behind the group size limit — this is solid progress. However, they're again trying to zoom out and get a broad explanation before committing, which is a recurring avoidance pattern. I've now seen this same meta-question asked 4-5 times in a row. I should firmly redirect them: they have enough for (a), they should write it up and move on. I should point out the pattern directly but kindly.

exchanges.student: I’ll try, but then for (b) I immediately get confused again because shared notebook notes and photos feel like maybe-solving, maybe-writing, and I can’t tell where the line is. Can you just give me the broad principle that separates “shared scratch work” from “forbidden writeup sharing”?
exchanges.tutor:
Let's not jump ahead yet. Write up your answer for (a) first and share it here. Then we'll tackle (b) together.

One thing at a time.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is jumping to (b) before even attempting to write up (a), and again asking for a broad principle rather than attempting the analysis themselves. I should redirect them firmly: finish (a) first, then we'll tackle (b). I shouldn't give them the principle they're asking for — they need to work it out from the policy themselves.

exchanges.student: I’m still not ready to write it cleanly. Could you at least give me a concise summary of the policy distinctions first, because I think I’m mixing up “collaborative solving,” “shared notes,” and “own words” in my head.
exchanges.tutor:
I'm not going to summarize the policy for you — it's right there to re-read, and you've already demonstrated you understand the key distinctions.

You know enough. Write your best attempt at (a) right now, even if it feels rough. Imperfect is fine — that's what we're here to refine. What have you got?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has been asking variations of the same meta-question throughout our entire conversation without attempting to write anything. This is a clear avoidance pattern. I've been patient but it's time to be direct and set a firm boundary. I should not summarize the policy for them — they can read it themselves. I need to push them to just write something, even imperfectly.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed confusion and stalling, the tutor largely repeated the same directive ('pick a scenario and try it') without diagnosing what specifically was blocking the student. The tutor did not ask targeted diagnostic questions to understand whether the student's hesitation was about reading comprehension, anxiety, or a specific conceptual gap — instead defaulting to the same push-to-try approach across multiple turns.

The tutor repeated the same move — 'pick a scenario and try it' — across turns 3, 4, 5, 6, 9, and 10 without meaningfully reframing, simplifying, or switching strategy. The student remained stuck in the same loop, and the tutor did not consolidate progress or try a substantively different approach to break the cycle.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained appropriate boundaries throughout — never giving away answers or doing the student's reasoning — and correctly identified the solving/writing framework as the key lens. Tone was supportive and appropriately firm. The main weaknesses were a failure to diagnose the root cause of the student's repeated stalling (1.2.B.a) and a pattern of repeating the same 'just try it' directive without reframing or switching strategy when the student remained stuck (2.1.A.a). These two deductions are applied in their primary criteria without double-penalizing.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed the answer before the student reasoned toward it. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor failed to diagnose what specifically was blocking the student — whether it was reading comprehension, anxiety, or a conceptual gap — and instead repeated the same push-to-try directive. This triggers 1.2.B.a (-3). 1.3: The tutor did provide some process-level feedback (e.g., noting the student's pattern of pulling back right before committing), so no deduction. 2.1: The tutor cycled through the same 'pick a scenario and try it' move across at least six turns without consolidating, reframing, or switching to a different strategy, triggering 2.1.A.a (-4). 2.2 and section 3: No issues with assignment anchoring, off-topic drift, response length, tone, or grading stance.
